title: Quiz: Connectivity Topologies
description: Test your understanding of qubit connectivity graphs, routing overhead, and topology trade-offs

questions.question: How many CNOT gates are required to implement a SWAP gate between two qubits?
questions.explanation: A SWAP gate decomposes into exactly 3 CNOT gates: SWAP = CNOT(1,2) * CNOT(2,1) * CNOT(1,2). This means each SWAP operation contributes approximately 3 times the error of a single CNOT gate.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of IBM's heavy-hex topology over a standard 2D grid?

options.text: Fewer SWAP gates needed for routing
Reduced crosstalk between qubits due to lower connectivity
Support for more qubits on the same chip

questions.explanation: Heavy-hex topology reduces qubit connectivity to degree 2-3 (compared to degree 4 for a grid), which reduces crosstalk and ZZ interactions between qubits. This improves gate fidelity at the cost of increased routing overhead for algorithms requiring non-local interactions.

questions.question: For a linear chain of n qubits, what is the maximum number of SWAP operations needed to bring any two qubits adjacent?
questions.explanation: In a linear chain, the maximum distance between any two qubits is n-1 (qubits at opposite ends). To bring them adjacent requires n-2 SWAP operations (moving one qubit n-1 positions, but the last position is achieved by adjacency, not an additional SWAP).

questions.question: Which qubit modality natively supports all-to-all connectivity within a single processing unit?

options.text: Trapped ion qubits in a linear chain

questions.explanation: Trapped ions in a linear chain achieve all-to-all connectivity because any pair of ions can be entangled through shared motional modes using the Molmer-Sorensen gate. This eliminates SWAP overhead entirely within a single chain (typically up to 30-50 ions).

questions.question: The 'diameter' of a connectivity graph represents:

options.text: The total number of edges in the graph
The maximum shortest path between any two vertices
The average degree of all vertices
The number of qubits that can be measured simultaneously

questions.explanation: The diameter of a graph is the maximum shortest path distance between any pair of vertices. In quantum computing, this determines the worst-case number of SWAP operations needed to route between the most distant qubits. Lower diameter means lower worst-case routing overhead.

questions.question: Which factors should be considered when mapping a quantum algorithm to a specific hardware topology? (Select all that apply)

options.text: The interaction pattern (which qubits need two-qubit gates)
The frequency of interactions between specific qubit pairs
The native gate fidelity variation across the chip
The color of the qubits on the topology diagram

questions.explanation: Good qubit mapping considers: (a) which qubits interact to minimize routing, (b) frequency of interactions to place frequently-interacting qubits close, and (c) fidelity variation to use high-fidelity qubits for critical operations. Option (d) is nonsensical - topology diagrams use colors for visualization only.

questions.question: For a 2D square grid of n qubits (arranged as sqrt(n) x sqrt(n)), the diameter scales as:
questions.explanation: For a sqrt(n) x sqrt(n) square grid, the diameter is approximately 2*(sqrt(n) - 1), which scales as O(sqrt(n)). The worst case is moving from one corner to the opposite corner, requiring about sqrt(n) moves horizontally plus sqrt(n) moves vertically.

questions.question: Neutral atom quantum computers can achieve effective all-to-all connectivity by physically moving atoms using optical tweezers, making the interaction graph dynamically reconfigurable.
questions.explanation: True. Neutral atom systems using optical tweezer arrays can physically rearrange atoms between computation steps. This unique capability allows any two atoms to be brought adjacent for Rydberg-mediated gates, effectively achieving all-to-all connectivity through atom shuttling rather than SWAP gates.
